coredata.dc:title: A facile method to sensitively monitor chlorinated phenols based on Ru(bpy)3 2+ electrochemiluminescent system using graphene quantum dots as coreactants
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Bao-Ping
Zhang, Xiaoru
Shang, Bing-Bing
Xiang, Dongshan
Qu, Wanyun
Zhang, Shenghui
coredata.dc:description: Except the tunable photoluminescent property, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with special structures have also exhibited other novel properties. Herein, GQDs played a novel coreactant role in Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system to increase the anodic ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+, which has been testified by ultraviolet, fluorescence, and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) experiments. Based on the coreactant mechanism of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system, a chlorinated phenol-related quenching ECL sensor was designed and the sensor showed a good linear relationship in the range from 0.60 to 55 μM with the detection limit of 1.4 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3). These results were not only beneficial to resolve the structure of GQDs, but also expanded the application field of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Ru(bpy)3 2+
Electrochemiluminescence
Detection
Phenol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image of GQDs. (b) The bright-field and fluorescence photographs of GQDs. (c) Size distribution histogram of GQDs. (d) UV–vis and PL spectra of GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The XPS (a) and FT-IR (b) spectra of graphene and GQDs, respectively.

CVs (a) and ECL curves (b) of GCE in 0.2 M pH 7.2 PBS containing 50 ng/mL GQDs (balck curve), 0.2 μM Ru(bpy)3 2+ (blue curve), and 50 ng/mL GQDs with 0.2 μM Ru(bpy)3 2+ (green curve), respectively. The potential cycled between +0.2 V and +1.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scanning rate of 0.1 V/s and the photomultiplier tube biased at 900 V. (c) CVs of GCE in 0.2 M pH 7.2 PBS with 0.15 mg/mL GQDs (solid line) and without GQDs (dash line) at 0.1 V/s. (d) The normalized PL spectrum of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (hollow circle), and ECL spectra of GQDs (solid triangle) and Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs (solid square). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effect of the working electrode (a) and atmosphere (b) on the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system. (c) The effect of pH value on the electrochemical behaviors of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system. (d) The effect of pH value on ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) The quenching effect of CP at 0, 0.6, 1.8, 3.8, 6, 16, and 55 μM; (b) The linear fitting of I0/I versus the concentration of CP. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Proposed pathway for anodic ECL of Ru(bpy)3 2+ coreacted by GQDs.

(a) C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of GQDs (black curve) and R-GQDs (navy curve), respectively. (b) The anodic ECL curves of the Ru(bpy)3 2+ solutions containing GQDs (black curve) and R-GQDs (navy curve), respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison of different methods for the detection of CP.
Recovery study for CP with various real water samples.

row.entry: Proposed method mean a ± SD b (10−7 M)

head.ce:title: A facile method to sensitively monitor chlorinated phenols based on Ru(bpy)3 2+ electrochemiluminescent system using graphene quantum dots as coreactants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biologic Resources Protection and Utilization, College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Hubei University for Nationalities, Enshi 445000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biologic Resources Protection and Utilization
College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Except the tunable photoluminescent property, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with special structures have also exhibited other novel properties. Herein, GQDs played a novel coreactant role in Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system to increase the anodic ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+, which has been testified by ultraviolet, fluorescence, and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) experiments. Based on the coreactant mechanism of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system, a chlorinated phenol-related quenching ECL sensor was designed and the sensor showed a good linear relationship in the range from 0.60 to 55 μM with the detection limit of 1.4 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3). These results were not only beneficial to resolve the structure of GQDs, but also expanded the application field of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), characterized by surface oxygenous functional groups, have recently exhibited a great potential in biosensing and bioimaging for its small size, low cost, superior photoluminescence (PL), electrochemiluminescence (ECL), excellent biocompatibility, etc. [1–7] The synthesis methods, properties, biological applications, and comparisons with other QDs (such as carbon QDs) of GQDs have been well summarized in these comprehensive reviews [6–8]. In recent years, the ECL of GQDs has been extensively studied and shown an increasing trend [9]. GQDs could be used as ECL labeling signals for immunoassays [9–13]. Especially, GQDs could also catalyze or amplify other ECL luminophor such as CdS [14], Ru(bpy)3 2+ [15,16], and luminol [17]. This property was generally attributed to the surface oxygen on GQDs [18], since most of oxygen-containing compounds (e.g., alcohols) have been reported to serve as the coreactants in ECL [19–21]. However, little research has been done regarding what the exact surface functional groups on GQDs were and how they resulted in the corresponding properties.

Chlorinated phenols were a typical group of persistent organic pollutants in the environment, and several of them have been listed as priority pollutants by the US Environmental Protection Agency and classified as a group 2B environmental carcinogen by International Agency for Research on Cancer. Extensive work has been carried out on the trace detection of chlorinated phenols in the past decade [22–24]. However, those methods required bulky equipment and tedious sample preparation. ECL technique represented the marriage of electrochemistry with spectroscopy and became a powerful analytical technique with the merits of rapidity, high sensitivity, and selectivity [20,21,25,26]. GQDs-based ECL sensing was a powerful detection method and has been widely reported for the detection of chemical and biological species. However, reports on the environmental detection by adopting GQDs-based ECL sensing were relatively scarce [27], though it had the merits of miniaturization, inexpensive instrumentation, excellent detection sensitivity, and high selectivity.

Herein, GQDs were introduced into the well-studied Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL system. The purpose of the present study was to reveal the role that GQDs played in the anodic ECL of Ru(bpy)3 2+, and develop more efficient and environmentally friendly coreactants for Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL assays. This work has provided an insight into the knowledge of GQDs, which would be beneficial for its further application.

Graphene sheets and Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd and Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Corporation, respectively. All other reagents were analytical grade and obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water used in the experiment was from a Synergy ultrapure water system (Millipore).

Graphene sheets (10 mg) were oxidized in piranha solution (10 mL, VH2SO4: V H2O2 = 7: 3) at 80 °C for 1 h (Caution: piranha solution is highly corrosive). Then the obtained green mixture was diluted with deionized water (250 mL) and adjusted to neutrality with sodium hydroxide in the ice-water bath. After that, the resulting solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1 kDa molar mass cutoff) for 5 days to remove the salt. After concentration, the strongly fluorescent part with nominal molecular weight of <3 kDa was obtained via ultrafilter and denoted as GQDs.

For the reduction of GQDs, excess sodium borohydride (0.1 g) was added into an aqueous GQDs (∼1.0 × 10−6 g/mL 100 mL) and stirred gently overnight at room temperature. Then, dialysis was adopted to remove the excess reductant as mentioned above.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were performed on a JEOL JEM-2100F high-resolution field-emission transmission electron microscope. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were taken using a SPM-9500J3 AFM by the tapping mode on the mica substrate. FT-IR spectra were obtained on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) spectra were collected using a VG Multilab 2000 XPS. Raman spectra were obtained from 1200 to 3000 cm−1 using a confocal Raman microspectroscopy (Renishaw RM-1000, excitation at 514 nm).

The ECL measurements were conducted on a MPI-E II ECL analytical system (Xi'an Remex Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd.) with a three-electrode configuration including a glass carbon working electrode, a Pt-wire counter electrode, and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode. By using cyclic voltammetry within the potential region of −1.5–0 V at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1, ECL signals of GQDs and 0.2 μM Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the electrolyte containing phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.2 M, pH 7.2) were obtained and collected simultaneously by the photomultiplier tube (PMT) biased at 900 V. The ECL spectrum was obtained by collecting the ECL peak data at + 1.13 V with 10 pieces of long-pass filters from 700 to 455 nm between the cell and photomultiplier.

GQDs were prepared according to the previous report with a little modification [28]. TEM image suggested that uniform and monodisperse GQDs with an average diameter of (2.8 ± 0.2) nm (statistical analysis of at least 200 GQDs) were obtained (Fig. 1 a and c). HR-TEM images (Fig. S1a) indicated that the lattice spacing of GQDs was about 2.13 Å and 1.80 Å, corresponding to the 100 and 102 facet of graphite (PDF#65-6212), respectively. AFM measurements showed that the topographic heights of GQDs were both about 1 nm (Fig. S1b), indicating that most of GQDs were present in a single layer. GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent properties with the maximum emission peak at 497 nm (Fig. 1d). Under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, GQDs showed bright blue-green PL (Fig. 1b) with a quantum yield of 7.4% (Quinine sulfate as reference). XPS showed that the oxygen-to-carbon ratio of graphene and GQDs was 0.14 and 0.61, respectively. As shown in Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. 2 a), GQDs displayed the stretching vibrations of OH (3435 cm−1), CH (2925 and 2850 cm−1), COOH (1723 cm−1), CO (1618 cm−1), and CO (1096 cm−1), along with the rocking vibrations of CH (1433 and 1338 cm−1). Both FT-IR and XPS spectra showed that the density of oxygenous groups on GQDs was obviously increased in comparison with the starting material graphene. Besides, some unsaturated groups, such as CC, were also detected on GQDs surface from the Uv–vis absorption (Fig. 1d). GQDs showed a continuously rising absorption with a defined peak at 290 nm, which was assigned to the π-π* transition of the conjugation system within GQDs. The Raman spectrum (Fig. S2) also provided convincing evidence of the conjugation system within GQDs. The relative intensity of the “disorder” D-band and the crystalline G-band (ID/IG) for as-produced GQDs in this work was only around 0.840, similar to that of GQDs fabricated from graphite intercalation compounds (0.873) [29]. It indicated the high quality of as-prepared GQDs, which was attributed to the proper oxidation system.

We have recently reported a solvothermal transformation of graphene to GQDs in piranha solution. More specifically, a pair of reversible redox peaks with the formal potential located at 0.29 V and an irreversible oxidation peak located at 0.85 V were detected by electrochemical method (Fig. 3 c). The reversible redox peaks have already been attributed to ortho-quinone derivatives at the edge of GQDs in our previous work [28]. Based on the specific reaction between ortho-quinones and 1,2-diamines, an efficient edge-functionalization strategy has been successfully employed to tune the PL properties of GQDs. In this work, we are not only interested in the origin of the irreversible oxidation peak from GQDs, but also its related application.

Herein, GQDs were introduced into the typical Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL system. Their electrochemical behaviors and ECL signals were recorded at the glass carbon electrode (GCE). As shown in Fig. 3a, GQDs alone exhibited an irreversible redox located at 0.85 V (black curve). Ru(bpy)3 2+ alone showed a reversible redox with a formal potential of +1.07 V (blue curve). After the addition of GQDs, the oxidation peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was increased while the reduction peak was almost disappeared (green curve). This indicated a catalytic oxidation of GQDs by electrogenerated Ru(bpy)3 3+. The similar catalytic oxidations were also observed in the coreactant ECL systems Ru(bpy)3 2+/tripropylamine (TPrA) [30], where TPrA was first catalytically oxidized to TPrA+• by electrogenerated Ru(bpy)3 3+. The ECL curves showed that the mixture Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs exhibited a strong ECL signal rising at + 1.00 V and peaking at + 1.13 V (Fig. 3b, green curve), which was consistent with the electrochemistry of Ru(bpy)3 2+. However, Ru(bpy)3 2+ (blue curve) or GQDs (black curve) alone showed weak anodic ECL signals. Furthermore, the ECL spectrum of the mixture at 600 nm agreed with the PL spectrum of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at 605 nm but distinguished from the ECL spectrum of GQDs alone at 490 nm (Fig. 3d). These results suggested that the anodic ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system originated from Ru(bpy)3 2+ and enhanced by GQDs.

The ECL luminophor was further confirmed by UV–vis absorption and PL emission. In the range of 280–700 nm, GQDs exhibited a rising absorption with an obvious peak at 290 nm (Fig. S3a). The mixture Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs showed a sharp peak at 290 nm and a broad peak at 453 nm (green curve), which was a merging of absorptions from separated GQDs (black curve) and Ru(bpy)3 2+ (blue curve). Likewise, Ru(bpy)3 2+ and GQDs in the mixture also kept their PL properties (Fig. S3b). These suggested that no reaction occurred between the excited-state or ground-state GQDs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecule. Thus, the light emission should result from the reaction between the electrogenerated Ru(bpy)3 3+ and GQDs.

From the above discussion, Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the system has been determined as the luminophor. GQDs could improve the ECL intensity of the luminophor, which probably acted as the coreactants. It could be verified by the linearly increasing ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ with increasing the concentration of GQDs from 2.0 to 80 ng/mL (Fig. S4). In order to improve the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system, several key factors have been investigated. Firstly, the effect of atmosphere on the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs was proceeded under oxygen, nitrogen, and air, respectively (Fig. 4 a). Compared to air atmosphere, the ECL intensity was further improved under nitrogen. It indicated that ECL signal was severely suppressed by the dissolved oxygen, due to an energy-transfer quench of the excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+∗ by the dissolved oxygen in aqueous solution. This conclusion was further confirmed by the effect of different working electrodes. As shown in Fig. 4b, the largest ECL intensity was achieved on GCE, while the minimum ECL intensity generated at Pt electrode. It was attributed to the lowest evolution potential of oxygen at Pt electrode. Then, the effect of the buffer pH on ECL intensity of the mixture was studied. As shown in Fig. 4c, with the increase of pH, the oxidation peak from GQDs was gradually decreased. It indicated that the alkaline conditions were in favor of the deprotonation process, and further beneficial to the oxidation process. The oxidation potential of Ru(bpy)3 2+ remained unchanged. At the same time, the peak of current dramatically increased with the pH value increased. As shown in Fig. 4d, the ECL intensity correspondingly increased sharply with pH increasing from 5 to 10, indicating that the deprotonation as a key step was existed in the ECL process. It provided good evidence for the ECL mechanism suggested in Fig. 6.

The most employed ECL system Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPrA had the characteristics of luminescence efficiency, reversible electrochemical behavior, chemical stability and versatility [20,21]. However, TPrA had several disadvantages: not only it is toxic, corrosive, and volatile, but also needs to be used in high concentrations (usually up to 100 mm) to obtain good sensitivity [31]. Since GQDs as a coreactants obeyed to the stringent requirements, such as good solubility, high chemical stability, and matching redox potentials, the ECL system Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs would have a great potential to displace the most employed system Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPrA in bioanalytical applications. The anodic ECL signal from the system Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs exhibited a robust stability during the 15 continuous cycles (Fig. S5) and the excellent 1-week repeatability with a relative standard deviation of 2.1%, providing a premise for bioanalysis. To further demonstrate this potency, we chose 4-chlorophenol (CP) as a model to demonstrate the potential of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system in ECL sensors. After adding CP into the 0.2 M PBS (pH 7.2) containing 0.2 μM Ru(bpy)3 2+ and 0.1 mg/mL GQDs, the anodic ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs system at GCE showed a significant decrease (Fig. 5 a). The ECL intensity decreased linearly with the increasing concentration of CP ranging from 0.60 to 55 μM (Fig. 5b), which followed the Stern-Volmer equation, I0/I = 1 + K SV [Q], where I0 was the initial ECL intensity, I was the ECL intensity at a given concentration of quencher [Q], and K SV was the quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer equation was widely used to express the relationship between ECL and quencher, and the constant Ksv of the system was 2.2 × 105 M−1 (n = 11, R = 0.998). The limit of detection was 1.4 × 10−8 M (S/N = 3), which was lower than most of the methods listed in Table 1 and was comparable to that of Bai's work [32].

The selectivity of the ECL sensor was also investigated by measuring the ECL responses to the addition of some common cations, anions, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (2,4,6-TNP), 4-dioxybenzene (DOB), and CPs, respectively. As shown in Table 2 , the ECL sensor showed high selectivity to CP, compared with the ECL inhibition ratio of the common ions (cations and anions) and the nonchlorinated phenols (2,4,6-TNP and DOB).

In order to investigate the capability of the method, CP in natural media was detected. The concentration of CP was detected by our method and GC-MS. Lake water sample was obtained from East Lake in Wuhan, China and River water sample was collected from Qingjiang River in Enshi, China. The nature samples were firstly filtered through 0.22 μm membranes to remove impurities, and then, the extraction solutions were tested using the proposed method and GC-MS, respectively. The testing results of CP in the lake and the river soil by the proposed method were in good agreement with those achieved by GC-MS (Table 3 ), indicating that the ECL sensing system could be employed in the identification of CP in the environment.

As discussed, it was essential to explore the substance of GQDs as the coreactants. The similar irreversible oxidation peak has also been observed in the direct voltammetry of colloidal graphene oxides (GO) [36,37], and the surface oxygen groups on GOs were suggested to be the active sites. However, their exact origins remained unclear. In our work the irreversible oxidation peak also should derive from the oxidation of a certain class of oxygenous functional groups on GQDs. Since the electrochemical behaviors of this peak were closely related with pH, alcohols and phenols were most probably. Most of oxygen-containing compounds (e.g. phenols, alcohols, aldehydes, carboxylic acid) have been found to be able to quench or enhance the ECL intensities of the Ru(bpy)3 2+-related coreactant ECL systems [19,21,38]. Phenols and its derivatives could significantly quench the ECL of oxidative-reduction type of coreactant ECL systems via a mechanism involving the energy or electron transfer [39,40]. However, alcohols and its derivatives have been reported to serve as the coreactants in ECL [38,41]. In the process, alcohols and its derivatives could be converted to alkoxide radical after one-electron oxidation. The alkoxide radical would then reduce electrogenerated Ru(bpy)3 3+ to form the excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+* [38,41]. Thus, the oxygenous units of alcohols on GQDs were proposed to serve as the coreactant sites, which could increase the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+. The mechanism for Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs ECL reactions was suggested in Fig. 6 . During the anodic scan, the alcohol units on GQDs were oxidized by electrogenerated Ru(bpy)3 3+ or direct electro-oxidation, turned to the related reductive intermediate upon a subsequent deprotonation, and further reduced Ru(bpy)3 3+ to Ru(bpy)3 2+∗, producing an anodic ECL emission. Since the potential of GQDs was close to that of Ru(bpy)3 2+, it provided favorable conditions for the radical reaction.

To further prove the key role of alcohol units, we further reduced GQDs by sodium borohydride, which selectively reduced the carbonyl and epoxy moieties to hydroxyl groups [42]. After reduction with sodium borohydride, the amount of hydroxyl groups on the surface of reduced GQDs (R-GQDs) significantly increased, which were characterized by FT-IR and XPS. The carbonyl stretching vibration (1618 cm−1) of R-GQDs was sharply weakened compared with that of GQDs (Fig. S6). XPS analysis also demonstrated that the carbonyl peak at 288.5 eV decreased in intensity after reduction, whereas the sp2 CC peak at 284.4 eV retained its original intensity (Fig. 7 a). It proved that GQDs were selectively reduced by sodium borohydride. As shown in Fig. 7b, when using the as-prepared R-GQDs instead of GQDs, the anodic ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/R-GQDs increased a lot (Fig. 7b, dotted), which was attributed to the abundant alcohol units on the surface of R-GQDs.

In summary, GQDs have been demonstrated to be the coreactants for the anodic ECL of Ru(bpy)3 2+. The alcohol units on GQDs were suggested to be active sites for the coreactants. Based on this coreactants system, a type of quenching sensor for the target CP was successfully fabricated. Since GQDs possess the merits of small size, water dispersibility, biocompatibility, and ease to be functionalized, the coreactants system of Ru(bpy)3 2+/GQDs would be much promising in bioanalysis. This work has deepened and broadened the knowledge of GQDs, which would be quite beneficial for further development of GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21361009, 21275003, 21465010 21465009, and 21175033), and the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Hubei University for Nationalities (MY2015B019).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.05.045.

Electrochemiluminescence emission from carbon quantum dot-sulfite coreactant system

Visible light photoredox catalysis with transition metal complexes: applications in organic synthesis
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence and its biorelated applications
Chlorinated phenol analysis using off-line solid-phase extraction and capillary electrophoresis coupled with amperometric detection and a boron-doped diamond microelectrode

Supported liquid membrane work-up in combination with liquid chromatography and electrochemical detection for the determination of chlorinated phenols in natural water samples

Tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II)-bisoprolol-based electrochemiluminescence coupled with capillary zone electrophoresis

An efficient edge-functionalization method to tune the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots
Highly efficient light-emitting diode of graphene quantum dots fabricated from graphite intercalation compounds
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence: an oxidative-reduction type ECL reaction sequence using tripropyl amine
Environmentally friendly and highly sensitive ruthenium(II) tris(2,2'-bipyridyl) electrochemiluminescent system using 2-(dibutylamino) ethanol as co-reactant

An ultrasonically facilitated boron-doped diamond voltammetric sensor for analysis of the priority pollutant 4-chlorophenol

A highly selective electrochemical sensor for 4-chlorophenol determination based on molecularly imprinted polymer and PDDA-functionalized graphene
Unusual inherent electrochemistry of graphene oxides prepared using permanganate oxidants
Direct voltammetry of colloidal graphene oxides
Highly efficient quenching of coreactant electrogenerated chemiluminescence by phenolic compounds

Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
